name: France
summary: Acute AI/software shortage and full spouse work rights on the Talent permit are the biggest levers, but a 47.3% Bangladeshi French-visa refusal rate, study years not counting toward PR/citizenship, a B2-French + civic-exam citizenship bar, and 2025-26 political instability with rising anti-Muslim incidents are heavy drags.

links.title: France-Visas – International talents
Campus France – Tuition fees in France

job-market.summary: Acute SW/AI shortage (~400k tech talent gap, 15k+ cyber roles unfilled); Paris SE ~€45-140k; Talent permit eases sponsorship; English in scale-ups.
job-market.reasoning: France has a severe software/AI talent shortage — an estimated 400,000 unfilled tech roles, 15,000+ cybersecurity posts open in 2025, and AI specialists among the hardest hires, so both engineers can place. Paris is the dominant hub (~89% of AI engineer openings in top cities) with software-engineer pay ~€45-65k junior to €95-140k+ senior and US-HQ firms (Datadog, Stripe) reaching €120-180k. The Talent – Qualified Employee permit (master's + contract, €39,582 floor) makes employer sponsorship routine, though many roles still expect working French and English-only jobs cluster in scale-ups/multinationals.
evidence: ~400,000 unfilled tech positions; 15,000+ cybersecurity roles open; AI specialists among hardest to recruit (2025)
Paris software engineer ~€45-65k junior to €95-140k+ senior; US-HQ offices €120-180k senior TC
Paris holds ~89% of AI-engineer openings among France's top-5 cities; median time-to-hire ~33 days
Talent – Qualified Employee permit: master's degree + 3-month+ contract, salary floor €39,582 (decree 29 Aug 2025)
AI engineer Paris average ~€54-80k; national average ~€80k

Welcome to France – Talent: Qualified employees

visa-access.summary: Processed in Dhaka via Campus France, €90 fee — but France refused 47.3% of Bangladeshi applicants; BD overall Schengen refusal 54.9% in 2024.
visa-access.reasoning: Long-stay student/Talent applications are handled in Dhaka through Campus France/Études en France and the French mission, so no New Delhi travel is needed, and fees are low (€90 long-stay; ~€615/month proof). But Bangladesh is one of the highest-refusal nationalities: France refused 47.3% of Bangladeshi applicants in 2024 and Bangladesh's overall Schengen refusal hit 54.9% (up from 43.3% in 2023, vs ~14.8% global) — signalling heavy document scrutiny, even though long-stay academic vetting differs from short-stay Schengen.
evidence: Campus France two-step (academic review + visa section) decided in Dhaka; biometrics at the mission/VFS
Long-stay student visa fee €90; financial proof ~€615/month
France refused 47.3% of Bangladeshi Schengen applications in 2024 (highest issuer rate for BD)
Bangladesh overall Schengen refusal 54.9% in 2024, up from 43.3% in 2023 (vs ~14.8% global average)
France overall Schengen refusal only 15.8% in 2024 — BD applicants face ~3x the average

links.title: Bangladesh Campus France – Documents for visa
HelloSafe – Schengen visa rejection barometer 2024-2025

citizenship.summary: Dual nationality allowed (BD permits via DNC) but B2 French + 40-Q civic exam (80%) from Jan 2026; 5yr residence excludes study years; ~18-24mo processing.
citizenship.reasoning: France permits dual nationality and Bangladesh's 2023 gazette lets BD nationals keep their passport via a Dual Nationality Certificate, matching the couple's no-renunciation preference. But from 1 Jan 2026 naturalisation needs B2 French (oral and written, up from B1) plus a 40-question civic exam (45 min, 80% to pass), the five years of habitual residence excludes time on a student permit, and naturalisations fell ~28% in 2024 under stricter prefecture circulars — pushing the realistic path well past five years plus heavy B2 prep.
evidence: Five years' habitual residence required; time on a student titre de séjour does NOT count
France recognises dual nationality; Bangladesh allows retention via Dual Nationality Certificate (2023 gazette, 101 countries incl. Europe)
From 1 Jan 2026: B2 French (oral + written) required, up from B1 (decrees of 15 Jul 2025)
New civic exam: 40 multiple-choice questions, 45 minutes, 80% to pass
Naturalisations granted fell ~28% in 2024 after stricter circulars; decree typically 18-24 months

post-study-work.summary: 12-month non-renewable APS/RECE for master's grads (24-month route is India-only); APS allows full-time work; switch to Talent once a contract is signed.
post-study-work.reasoning: Master's and engineering graduates get the APS/RECE job-search permit, which (unlike the student visa) allows full-time work — a genuine plus. But for Bangladeshi nationals it is the standard 12 months, non-renewable; the 24-month version is a bilateral concession limited to a few countries (e.g. India), so the couple does not benefit. Within that window they must find a job and switch to the Talent – Qualified Employee permit (master's + contract, €39,582 floor), a workable but time-pressured single bridge.
evidence: Standard APS/RECE for non-EU master's graduates: 12 months, non-renewable
24-month route applies only under bilateral agreements (e.g. India); Bangladesh gets 12 months
APS permits full-time employment, unlike the student visa's ~964h/yr cap
Allows switch to Talent – Qualified Employee permit once a qualifying contract is signed (€39,582 floor)

spouse-family.summary: Work phase: Talent–Famille gives spouse immediate full work rights, no salary floor. Study phase: accompanying spouse on visitor visa cannot work — a real gap.
spouse-family.reasoning: Once on a Talent permit, the Talent–Famille card gives the accompanying spouse immediate, unrestricted labour-market access with no salary threshold or separate sponsor — ideal for two engineers. But during the MSc study phase there is no clean route: a spouse arriving as a student's dependant typically gets a 'visiteur' visa that bars employment, and family reunification (which allows work) needs ~18 months' residence plus income and accommodation tests, so the second engineer is effectively idle through the study years.
evidence: Talent–Famille permit grants the spouse unrestricted labour-market access for the main permit's duration, no salary threshold
Study-phase accompanying spouse on a 'visiteur' visa is not authorised to work
Family reunification requires ~18 months' residence + minimum income + accommodation
Talent–Famille issued concurrently with the main Talent permit (no separate work-permit application)

msc-study.summary: 1,300+ English master's, Eiffel grants, 964h/yr (~21h/wk) work — but mandatory non-EU tuition jumps to €3,941/yr from Sept 2026 and spouse can't work then.
msc-study.reasoning: France offers 1,300+ English-taught master's programmes plus the Eiffel scholarship and student work rights of 964h/yr (~21h/week), far cheaper than Anglophone destinations. But from September 2026 differentiated non-EU tuition becomes mandatory at ~€3,941/yr for master's (about 16x the old €254), with exemptions capped near 10%, and the accompanying spouse cannot work during the study phase — both materially dent the value for this dual-income couple. Study years also do not count toward PR or citizenship.
evidence: 1,300+ English-taught degree programmes (Campus France); 1,000+ fully-English master's listed
Mandatory non-EU master's tuition ~€3,941/yr from Sept 2026 (vs ~€254 previously); ~16x increase
Exemptions capped near 10% (scholarship/hardship cases)
Students may work up to 964h/yr (~21h/week); France Excellence Eiffel scholarship available
Spouse on dependant 'visiteur' visa cannot work during studies

pr-pathway.summary: 10-yr carte de résident needs 5yrs continuous legal residence — but student years DON'T count, and B1 French + civic exam required from 2026.
pr-pathway.reasoning: The 10-year carte de résident generally needs five years' continuous legal residence with stable income and integration, and Talent holders have a recognised route. The catch for this couple is that student-permit time does not count toward the five years (confirmed for both PR and EU long-term residence), so the clock effectively starts only after they switch to a work/Talent permit — adding 2 years versus the prior assumption. From 1 Jan 2026 the resident card also requires B1 French plus a civic exam, and absences over six months can reset continuity.
evidence: Carte de résident (10-year) after ~5 years' continuous legal residence + stable income + integration
Student titre de séjour does NOT count toward the 5 years for PR or EU long-term residence (Service-Public)
From 1 Jan 2026: B1 French + civic exam required for the resident card (decrees of 15 Jul 2025)
Talent permit offers a recognised path to permanent residence; first multi-year card now requires A2 French

income-cost.summary: Paris SE pay ~€45-140k gross, net ~68-75% after employee charges; 1BR rent €1,200-2,000, target €3,000-3,500/mo to live comfortably — moderate savings.
income-cost.reasoning: Paris software engineers earn roughly €45-65k junior to €95-140k+ senior, and employee-side deductions (social charges + income tax) leave about 68-75% net — e.g. €75k gross nets ~€51k. But central Paris one-bedroom rent runs €1,200-2,000 and a comfortable single budget is €3,000-3,500/month, so two engineering incomes deliver moderate rather than strong savings; the family quotient mitigates tax for couples.
evidence: Paris software engineer ~€45-65k junior, €95-140k+ senior; Paris average ~€81k gross (broad samples)
Net take-home ~68-75% of gross (e.g. €75k gross → ~€51k net after tax + social charges)
Central Paris 1-bedroom rent ~€1,200-2,000/month; outer districts ~€900-1,400
Comfortable single budget in Paris ~€3,000-3,500 net/month
Dual income + quotient familial improves household savings potential

healthcare.summary: Universal PUMA after ~3mo residence; ~84% costs prepaid, ~70% base reimbursement; new €300-600/yr PUMA fee for non-EU visitor-visa holders from 2026.
healthcare.reasoning: France's Protection Universelle Maladie covers all legal residents after about three months and is among the widest-access systems in Europe (full population covered, 11.5% of GDP on health). The state reimburses ~70% of base rates so most residents add a mutuelle for full cover, and a new 2026 Social Security law imposes a €300-600/yr PUMA contribution on non-EU 'visiteur'-visa holders (relevant to the study-phase spouse). Satisfaction with availability is moderate (60% vs OECD 64%), so it is excellent but not flawless.
evidence: PUMA covers all legal residents; access after ~3 months' residence
France spent 11.5% of GDP on health in 2024 (2.2 pts above OECD avg); full population covered
State reimburses ~70% of base rates; most residents add a mutuelle for ~100% cover
2026 Social Security Financing Law: €300-600/yr PUMA fee for non-EU visitor-visa holders (decree pending)
Only 60% satisfied with availability of quality healthcare (OECD avg 64%)

culture-language.summary: English works in tech and study, but A2/B1/B2 French now layered across permits, resident card and citizenship; French needed for daily admin.
culture-language.reasoning: English is usable in tech workplaces and across 1,300+ English-taught programmes, but the 2026 reforms make French unavoidable along the whole pathway — A2 for the first multi-year card, B1 plus a civic exam for the resident card, B2 plus a 40-question civic exam for citizenship — and daily prefecture/admin life runs in French. The escalating language ladder is the dominant cultural barrier for this English-working couple, partly offset by strong work-life balance and a large diverse capital.
evidence: From 2026: A2 (first multi-year card), B1 + civic exam (resident card), B2 + civic exam (naturalisation)
Civic exam (40 Q, 80%) now required for both the resident card and naturalisation
French essential for daily life and prefecture/administrative dealings
English common in tech and across 1,300+ English-taught programmes (eases entry only)

Service-Public – Becoming French by naturalisation

safety-law.summary: WJP Rule of Law #22/143 (2025); strong institutions; urban petty theft and periodic protests the main day-to-day concerns.
safety-law.reasoning: France ranks 22nd of 143 on the 2025 WJP Rule of Law Index with solid order/security and fundamental-rights scores and an independent judiciary. Violent crime is relatively low; pickpocketing on the Paris metro and around major stations plus periodic protests are the practical risks. Rising anti-Muslim incidents are a fairness concern weighed mainly under muslim-diaspora, which tempers the score modestly.
evidence: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: France ranks 22 of 143
Index measures order & security, fundamental rights, civil & criminal justice — France solid across them
Paris crime concentrated in petty theft/pickpocketing; violent crime relatively low
Periodic protests/strikes and caution in parts of northern arrondissements after dark

politics.summary: Five PMs since 2022, repeated 2025-26 government collapses, hung Assembly, RN tops snap-election polls; cascading immigration tightening.
politics.reasoning: France is in chronic political instability: its fifth PM since 2022 (Lecornu) governs a hung Assembly that has seen repeated collapses and no-confidence fights over the 2026 budget, with the deficit at 5.8%. The far-right Rassemblement National tops polls for any snap election, and the 2024 immigration law plus cascading 2025-26 tightening (A2/B1 permit French, B2 + civic exam for citizenship, prefecture circulars cutting naturalisations ~28%) shift policy steadily against non-EU applicants — an unstable, unfavourable backdrop for this profile.
evidence: Fifth prime minister since 2022; repeated government collapses 2025-26; budget passed without a vote
Hung National Assembly split into three blocs; deficit ~5.8% (near double EU limit)
Rassemblement National polls first in any snap parliamentary election (Elabe 2026)
January 2024 immigration law (35 of 86 articles censured); cascading 2025-26 tightening of language/civic rules
Prefecture circulars cut naturalisations ~28% in 2024

tax.summary: Progressive IR 0-45% + CSG/CRDS 9.7% + pensions; engineer net ~68-75% of gross; quotient familial eases couples — heavy but not extreme.
tax.reasoning: France's 2026 income tax runs 0% to €11,600 then 11/30/41/45%, plus CSG/CRDS ~9.7% on 98.25% of gross and pension contributions, giving total employee social deductions of ~22-25%. For a typical Paris engineer net pay lands around 68-75% of gross (e.g. €75k → ~€51k net), among the heavier OECD regimes; the quotient familial (income split across household 'parts') meaningfully reduces the burden for a married couple.
evidence: 2026 IR brackets 0-45%; thresholds raised ~0.9% for inflation (2026 Finance Act); tax-free to €11,600/part
CSG 9.2% + CRDS 0.5% on 98.25% of gross; total employee social deductions ~22-25%
€75k gross → income tax ~€7,700 + social charges ~€16,200 → ~€51,100 net (~68%)
Quotient familial splits household income across 'parts' (2 for a couple), cutting effective tax

muslim-diaspora.summary: Largest Muslim community in Europe (~9%), halal/mosques widespread — but anti-Muslim acts +75% and assaults +209% in Jan-May 2025; strict laïcité.
muslim-diaspora.reasoning: France has Europe's largest Muslim population (~9%) with abundant halal food and mosques, easing daily religious life, and Paris hosts a sizeable South-Asian diaspora. But laïcité bans religious symbols in state schools (abayas since 2023) and across several sports, and hostility is climbing sharply — anti-Muslim acts rose 75% (83→145) and assaults 209% (32→99) in Jan-May 2025 vs 2024, with one in three Muslims reporting discrimination — a serious, worsening concern for a visibly Muslim couple that holds the score at the mixed band.
evidence: ~9% of population Muslim — largest Muslim community in Western Europe; halal and mosques widespread
Anti-Muslim acts +75% (83→145) and assaults +209% (32→99) in Jan-May 2025 vs 2024
1 in 3 Muslims in France report religious discrimination (2025)
Laïcité: hijab/abaya banned in state schools; headscarf banned across several sports; further bans debated 2025
Fatal mosque stabbing (Apr 2025) among high-profile incidents fuelling concern
